interpretations.title: Results and Interpretations
rows: Measured for size, shape, and position.
Normal size indicates healthy anatomy. Enlargement could suggest fibroids or adenomyosis.
The thickness of the uterine lining, measured in millimeters (mm).
Varies with the menstrual cycle. An abnormally thick or thin lining may require further investigation.
Assessed for size, and the presence of follicles or cysts.
The presence of developing follicles is normal in premenopausal women. Cysts are described by size and characteristics.
Presence of fluid in the pelvic cul-de-sac.
A small amount can be normal (physiologic). A large amount may indicate inflammation, infection, or other conditions.

faqs.question: Is a transvaginal ultrasound painful?
faqs.answer: No, it should not be painful. Most women describe the sensation as mild pressure, similar to a Pap test. If you feel pain, please let your sonographer know right away.

faqs.question: How long does the TVS procedure take?
faqs.answer: The scan itself typically lasts between 15 and 30 minutes. You may want to allow a little extra time for checking in and preparation.

faqs.question: Can I have a TVS scan during my period?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is absolutely safe to have a TVS scan during menstruation. In fact, for some medical questions, your doctor may specifically schedule it during this time. The presence of blood does not interfere with the quality of the ultrasound images.

faqs.question: What is the TVS scan cost at Cadabams Diagnostics?
faqs.answer: The **TVS scan cost** can vary. For the most accurate and up-to-date pricing information for the **Transvaginal Ultrasound (TVS)** at Cadabams Diagnostics, please contact our front desk directly.

faqs.question: When will I receive my results?
faqs.answer: The radiologist’s formal report is typically finalized and sent to your referring doctor within 24-48 hours. Your doctor will then schedule a follow-up with you to discuss the results.

seo.title: Transvaginal Ultrasound (TVS) | Cadabams Diagnostics
seo.description: Learn about the transvaginal ultrasound procedure at Cadabams Diagnostics. Find out what a TVS scan shows, how to prepare, and why this gynecological ultrasound is performed.

test.markdown:
## Transvaginal Ultrasound (TVS) Overview

A Transvaginal Ultrasound, commonly known as a TVS, is a safe, painless, and highly detailed diagnostic imaging test used to examine the female pelvic organs. At Cadabams Diagnostics, we understand that undergoing any medical procedure can raise questions. This guide is designed to provide you with a clear understanding of the \*\*Transvaginal Ultrasound (TVS)\*\*, its purpose, and what you can expect. Its primary function is to provide a much clearer and more detailed view of the uterus, ovaries, fallopian tubes, and cervix than a standard abdominal ultrasound. Because it is an internal procedure, the imaging probe gets closer to these organs, resulting in superior image quality for a more accurate diagnosis.
## What is a Transvaginal Ultrasound?
A \*\*Transvaginal Ultrasound\*\* is a type of pelvic ultrasound that utilizes safe, high-frequency sound waves to create real-time images of your internal pelvic structures. The technology involves a small, specialized probe, called a transducer, which is designed to be comfortable and effective.
During the scan, this probe is gently placed inside the vaginal canal. This proximity to the pelvic organs is the key advantage of a TVS, allowing it to bypass layers of skin, fat, and muscle that can sometimes obscure the view in an abdominal scan. The result is sharper, more distinct images of the uterus, ovaries, and cervix. This procedure is also frequently referred to as an \*\*endovaginal ultrasound\*\*, and the terms are used interchangeably in the medical community.
## Types of Transvaginal Ultrasound
### Standard 2D TVS
This is the most frequently performed type, providing flat, two-dimensional, cross-sectional images of the pelvic organs. It is excellent for general evaluation of size, shape, and structure.
### Sonohysterography
This is an enhanced \*\*Transvaginal Ultrasound\*\*. In this procedure, a sterile saline solution is gently infused into the uterus through a thin catheter. The fluid gently expands the uterine cavity, allowing for a much clearer visualization of the endometrial lining to detect polyps, fibroids, or other abnormalities.
### Doppler Ultrasound
A Doppler study is often performed as part of a TVS. This special technique assesses blood flow to and within the pelvic organs. It is particularly useful for evaluating ovarian cysts and tumors by characterizing their blood supply, which can help differentiate between benign and malignant masses.

## Risks & Limitations of the Transvaginal Ultrasound

### Risks
The \*\*Transvaginal Ultrasound (TVS)\*\* is an extremely safe procedure with virtually no risks. It does not use ionizing radiation, like X-rays or CT scans, making it safe even during pregnancy. The primary concern for most patients is potential discomfort. Some women may feel mild pressure during the insertion of the transducer. It is important to communicate with your sonographer at Cadabams Diagnostics; if you feel any pain, they can adjust the probe to make you more comfortable.
### Limitations
While a TVS provides excellent detail of the lower pelvic organs, it has some limitations. It may not be the appropriate procedure for patients who have never been sexually active or who have certain medical conditions. In these cases, an abdominal or transrectal ultrasound may be recommended instead. Furthermore, its view can be limited for assessing masses or structures that are located higher up in the pelvis or abdomen, outside the range of the probe.

## List of Parameters Considered During the Transvaginal Ultrasound

A common question patients have is, \*\*"what does a TVS scan show?"\*\* The detailed images allow the radiologist to assess several key parameters and structures within the pelvis:
\* \*\*Uterus:\*\* The scan meticulously measures the uterus for its size, shape, and orientation (e.g., anteverted or retroverted). The texture of the uterine muscle (myometrium) is examined for any abnormalities like fibroids (benign tumors) or adenomyosis (a condition where endometrial tissue grows into the uterine wall).
\* \*\*Endometrium (Uterine Lining):\*\* The thickness of the endometrium is a crucial measurement that naturally changes throughout the menstrual cycle. The TVS evaluates if the thickness is appropriate for the stage of your cycle and looks for any irregularities, polyps, or signs of abnormal tissue growth.
\* \*\*Ovaries:\*\* Both ovaries are identified and measured. The sonographer looks for the presence of developing follicles (normal in premenopausal women), cysts, or solid tumors. The appearance of the ovaries can provide essential clues for diagnosing conditions like PCOS.
\* \*\*Cervix:\*\* The cervix is examined for its appearance and length. Measuring cervical length is especially important during pregnancy to assess the risk of preterm labor.
\* \*\*Fallopian Tubes:\*\* Healthy fallopian tubes are usually not visible on an ultrasound. However, a TVS can detect if a tube is swollen or filled with fluid (a condition called hydrosalpinx), which can be a sign of infection or blockage.
\* \*\*Pelvic Area (Cul-de-sac):\*\* The area behind the uterus, known as the cul-de-sac, is checked for the presence of free fluid. While a small amount can be normal, an excessive amount might indicate inflammation, ovulation, or other conditions.

## Reasons for Taking a Transvaginal Ultrasound

Your doctor has a specific clinical question they are trying to answer when they order this test. Common reasons include:
\* Investigating gynecological symptoms like chronic pelvic pain, painful periods (dysmenorrhea), heavy bleeding (menorrhagia), or irregular cycles.
\* Identifying the cause of postmenopausal bleeding, which always requires investigation.
\* As a key part of an infertility workup, including monitoring the response of the ovaries to fertility medications (follicle tracking).
\* Confirming an early pregnancy, checking for a heartbeat, and ruling out a dangerous ectopic pregnancy (where the fetus implants outside the uterus).
\* Verifying the correct placement of an intrauterine device (IUD) for contraception.
\* Evaluating pelvic masses, such as ovarian cysts or uterine fibroids, that were found during a routine pelvic exam.
## When and Who Needs to Take a Transvaginal Ultrasound?
A \*\*Transvaginal Ultrasound\*\* is recommended by doctors for a wide range of diagnostic purposes. It is a cornerstone of gynecological health assessment. You may be advised to have a TVS if you are experiencing:
\* Unexplained pelvic pain
\* Abnormal or heavy menstrual bleeding
\* Bleeding after menopause
\* Suspected infertility issues
\* Symptoms of Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)
It is a crucial \*\*gynecological ultrasound scan\*\* for women of all ages who present with specific health concerns. Additionally, it is commonly used in the first trimester of pregnancy to confirm the pregnancy, check the fetal heartbeat, determine the gestational age, and rule out an ectopic pregnancy.

## Benefits of Taking the Test

Opting for a \*\*Transvaginal Ultrasound\*\* at Cadabams Diagnostics offers several key benefits for your health journey:
\* \*\*Superior Image Quality:\*\* Provides high-resolution, unambiguous images for a more confident and accurate diagnosis compared to other methods.
\* \*\*Safety:\*\* Involves no radiation exposure, making it completely safe for repeated examinations and for use during pregnancy without any risk to the fetus.
\* \*\*Informed Treatment Planning:\*\* The detailed findings help your doctor create the most effective treatment plan, whether it involves medication, monitoring, or surgical intervention.
\* \*\*Efficient Procedure:\*\* A TVS is a quick outpatient procedure, typically lasting under 30 minutes, providing immediate imaging for rapid assessment.

## Illnesses Diagnosed with Transvaginal Ultrasound

This powerful diagnostic tool is instrumental in identifying and characterizing a wide range of conditions, including:
\* \*\*Uterine Fibroids and Polyps:\*\* Benign growths in or on the uterus or its lining.
\* \*\*Ovarian Cysts and Tumors:\*\* Differentiating between simple, fluid-filled cysts and more complex or solid masses.
\* \*\*Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS):\*\* Identifying the characteristic "string of pearls" appearance of multiple small follicles on the ovaries.
\* \*\*Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID):\*\* Detecting signs of inflammation or abscesses in the pelvic region.
\* \*\*Endometriosis:\*\* While laparoscopy is the gold standard, TVS can identify specific signs, such as ovarian endometriomas ("chocolate cysts").
\* \*\*Ectopic Pregnancy:\*\* Crucial for early detection and immediate medical intervention.
\* \*\*Congenital Uterine Anomalies:\*\* Identifying structural abnormalities of the uterus present since birth.

## Preparing for Transvaginal Ultrasound

Preparation for a TVS is simple. Unlike an abdominal pelvic scan, where a full bladder is needed to push the bowel gas away, a \*\*Transvaginal Ultrasound\*\* requires the opposite.
\* \*\*Bladder:\*\* You will be asked to empty your bladder completely just before the scan. An empty bladder allows the pelvic organs to be seen more clearly with the internal probe.
\* \*\*Clothing:\*\* It is helpful to wear a comfortable, two-piece outfit (like a top and pants/skirt). This allows you to undress only from the waist down, keeping you more comfortable.
\* \*\*Menstrual Cycle:\*\* The scan can be performed at any time during your menstrual cycle. However, depending on the reason for your scan, your doctor may advise a specific time. For instance, to assess the endometrial lining, the best time is often right after your period ends.
## Pre-requisites
\* You will typically need a referral or prescription from your doctor to have a \*\*Transvaginal Ultrasound\*\*.
\* If you have had previous pelvic imaging (like another ultrasound or an MRI), it is very helpful to bring those reports with you for comparison.
## Best Time to Take the Transvaginal Ultrasound
The scan can be performed at any time. If your doctor is investigating infertility, the scan might be timed precisely to track follicle development and ovulation. For assessing the uterine lining for abnormalities like polyps, the ideal time is usually in the first half of the menstrual cycle, just after your period has finished. Your referring physician will provide guidance on the optimal timing for your specific situation.
## Eligibility
A TVS is generally performed on women who are sexually active. For patients who are virginal or cannot tolerate the internal probe for any reason, a transabdominal or transrectal ultrasound are excellent alternatives that can provide the necessary diagnostic information.
## Procedure for Taking a Transvaginal Ultrasound
Understanding the \*\*transvaginal ultrasound procedure\*\* step-by-step can help alleviate any anxiety. At Cadabams Diagnostics, our compassionate sonographers will guide you through the entire process.
### Step 1: Preparation
Upon arrival, you will be checked in. A sonographer will call you into the private ultrasound room, explain the procedure, and ask you to empty your bladder. You will then be asked to undress from the waist down and will be given a sheet or gown for privacy.
### Step 2: Positioning
You will be asked to lie on your back on a comfortable examination table, with your knees bent and your feet possibly placed in stirrups, similar to a gynecological exam.
### Step 3: The Transducer
The sonographer will show you the slender transducer probe. For your safety and hygiene, it will be covered with a new, sterile, disposable sheath. A water-based, hypoallergenic lubricating gel will be applied to the covered probe to allow for easy and comfortable insertion.
### Step 4: Insertion & Imaging
The sonographer will then gently insert the probe a few inches into your vagina. This should not be painful. They will then carefully move and angle the probe to capture clear images of your uterus, ovaries, and surrounding structures from multiple perspectives. You may be able to see the images on a screen during the scan.
### Step 5: Duration
The entire imaging process is usually completed in 15 to 30 minutes. Once all the necessary images are captured, the probe is removed, and you can get dressed.
## Caution Before Taking the Test
To ensure your safety and comfort, please remember to:
\* \*\*Inform the staff of any latex allergy.\*\* The standard probe covers contain latex, but non-latex alternatives are readily available.
\* \*\*Communicate any discomfort.\*\* If you experience any significant pain at any point, tell the sonographer immediately. The procedure should not be painful.
\* \*\*Confirm your pregnancy status.\*\* Always let the staff know if you are or might be pregnant.
